text: The surgeon exposes the gallbladder attached to the underside of the liver, beginning the dissection of surrounding tissue to clearly identify anatomical structures.
The surgeon clips the skeletonized cystic artery and cystic duct, securing these structures for subsequent division.
The surgeon creates a small hole in the cystic duct and advances a catheter into it, preparing to flush the ductal system with contrast dye visible under fluoroscopy.

A specialized instrument is used to detect the endocyanin green dye, providing an image of the hepatic duct system during the cholangiogram. The cholangiogram visually identifies any gallstones lodged in the duct.
The surgeon places clips on the cystic duct to close the hole made earlier, then proceeds to cut the remainder of the cystic duct and the cystic artery.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to detach the gallbladder from its light attachments to the liver. After the gallbladder is disconnected, the surgical team inspects the dissection area for any signs of bleeding before proceeding to remove the gallbladder from the body.

The surgeon prepares to remove the gallbladder by enlarging one incision if large or numerous stones are present. A retrieval bag is used to extract the gallbladder intact through the incision located to the right of the belly button.
